data.title: RSPS: Consumer Staples Dashboard For October
data.description:
This monthly article series reports industry metrics in the consumer staples sector. It is also a review of sector ETFs like The Consumer Staples Select Sector SPDR® Fund ETF (XLP) and Invesco S&P 500® Equal Weight Consumer Staples ETF (NYSEARCA:RSPS), whose holdings are used to calculate these metrics.

Shortcut

The next two paragraphs in italics describe the dashboard methodology. They are necessary for new readers to understand the metrics. If you are used to this series or if you are short of time, you can skip them and go to the charts.

Base Metrics

I calculate the median value of five fundamental ratios for each industry: Earnings Yield ("EY"), Sales Yield ("SY"), Free Cash Flow Yield ("FY"), Return on Equity ("ROE"), Gross Margin ("GM"). The reference universe includes large companies in the U.S. stock market. The five base metrics are calculated on trailing 12 months. For all of them, higher is better. EY, SY and FY are medians of the inverse of Price/Earnings, Price/Sales and Price/Free Cash Flow. They are better for statistical studies than price-to-something ratios, which are unusable or non-available when the "something" is close to zero or negative (for example, companies with negative earnings). I also look at two momentum metrics for each group: the median monthly return (RetM) and the median annual return (RetY).

I prefer medians to averages because a median splits a set into a good half and a bad half. A capital-weighted average is skewed by extreme values and the largest companies. My metrics are designed for stock-picking rather than index investing.

Value and Quality Scores

I calculate historical baselines for all metrics. They are noted respectively EYh, SYh, FYh, ROEh, GMh, and they are calculated as the averages on a look-back period of 11 years. For example, the value of EYh for food in the table below is the 11-year average of the median Earnings Yield in food companies.

The Value Score ("VS") is defined as the average difference in % between the three valuation ratios (EY, SY, FY) and their baselines (EYh, SYh, FYh). In the same way, the Quality Score ("QS") is the average difference between the two quality ratios (ROE, GM) and their baselines (ROEh, GMh).

The scores are in percentage points. VS may be interpreted as the percentage of undervaluation or overvaluation relative to the baseline (positive is good, negative is bad). This interpretation must be taken with caution: the baseline is an arbitrary reference, not a supposed fair value. The formula assumes that the three valuation metrics are of equal importance.

The next chart plots the Value and Quality Scores by industry (higher is better).

Evolution since last month

The value score has significantly deteriorated in the tobacco industry, and to a lesser extent in household products and staple/food retail. Quality has improved in tobacco, though.

Momentum

The next chart plots momentum based on median returns by subsector.

Interpretation

Based on my S&P 500 dashboard, the sector as a whole is close to its 11-year averages in valuation. The household products and beverage industries are quite attractive: they are respectively undervalued by 25% and 18%, and their quality score is close to the historical baseline. The tobacco industry is the least compelling subsector, with both value and quality scores significantly below the baseline.

Fast facts on RSPS

Invesco S&P 500 Equal Weight Consumer Staples ETF was listed on 11/01/2006 and tracks the S&P 500 Equal Weight Consumer Staples Index. It has 39 holdings and a total expense ratio of 0.40%, while the capital-weighted fund XLP charges only 0.09%. The gap looks considerable for rebalancing the same holdings on a quarterly basis.

All weights are equal after every rebalancing, but they drift with price action: they are now between 2.46% and 3.02%. The next table lists the current top 10 holdings, representing 27.7% of asset value. These are the constituents with the highest momentum since the last rebalancing. RSPS is better diversified than XLP, and risks related to individual companies are much lower. data.title: TransMedics Group: This Could Be One Of The Biggest Moats In A Decade
data.description:
The TransMedics Group Investment Thesis

I first became aware of TransMedics Group, Inc. (NASDAQ:TMDX) through social media earlier this year and purchased my first shares in April of this year. While this was a very small position for me, less than 1%, it is now a much larger part due to me adding shares and the favorable rise of the stock price.

What fascinated me about this company is that, in my opinion, they have an incredible product that helps to do something good, namely to extend or save lives. And I believe that they will continue to revolutionize the transplantation industry, and that is why I believe, as I will show later, that there is still a lot of upside potential and that TransMedics is a long-term buy and hold investment that I expect to do well over the next decade.

Business Model And Competition Position

Currently, TransMedics is the only FDA approved multi-organ platform with its three current offerings, OCS Lung, OCS Heart and OCS Liver. What makes TransMedics unique is that they have been able to keep organs alive longer than traditional methods, allowing the organs to travel greater distances. And because TransMedic's technology platform is designed to mimic the human body, it increases the number of organs that can actually be used.

If the organs are stored on ice and have no blood supply, this can lead to organ damage, and since the organs are not active during this time, it is not known whether they are damaged. So TransMedics has solved this problem and created an incredible competitive advantage, as clinical trials are very time-consuming and costly.

Currently, TransMedics is in the process of establishing a network of donor and transplant centers with which it will work on a long-term basis. This network could also be an important asset in the future to create further strong barriers to entry in addition to the technological lead.

In addition, it appears that TransMedics, through its acquired aviation division, is attempting to serve as the sole link between donor and transplant centers. This, in turn, is another step toward a stronger competitive advantage, as it means lower costs for customers, but also for TransMedics in the long run. A win-win situation.

By having its own fleet, the aircraft could soon be on standby 24 hours a day instead of just 12 hours, which is also positive for everyone involved. Especially when combined with the longer lifespan of donor organs, this results in fewer spontaneous night surgeries where the medical staff may not be 100% fit. Because anything that eliminates potential sources of error in the medical field will likely have a positive impact on surgical outcomes.

In my opinion, the more affordable transplantation becomes and the more healthy donor organs are available, the more transplantations will take place. Especially as the world's population continues to grow, and life expectancy continues to increase, the demand for transplants will also rise and is expected to lead to high growth rates. It is possible that TransMedics innovative product will even help fuel this growth.

In addition, there is a possibility that TransMedics will capture the kidney transplant market, which currently has the longest waiting list and where most transplants are performed. Plus, the aviation business could be growing faster than expected and TransMedics could be expanding internationally.

So I think TransMedics has great growth opportunities and is at a crossroads where they are laying the groundwork for the next decade. Another milestone is the inclusion of TransMedics in the S&P 600, which will lead to further inflows.

Valuation

Before we can start the valuation process, we need to take a look at the most recent earnings to get a better picture of the company. And here we can clearly see that the service or logistics segment has made a big jump due to the aircraft fleet. Unfortunately, this has also had an impact on margins, as service costs are significantly higher and I expect them to remain so for the next few years.

I am using 2028 as the endpoint of my valuation, as management has set a target of 10,000 transplants for FY2028. And I think that target should be achievable because it seems to me that management tends to be conservative, and therefore guidance tends to be set to be achievable.

So if TransMedics achieves 10,000 transplants at an average price of $105,000, they will achieve $1.05 billion in revenue, plus additional revenue, which I estimate at about $300 million. This would result in total revenues of $1.35 billion for FY28. As a net income margin, I use 15% because it gives me a margin of safety, but I think there is upside potential here because a higher net income margin is not out of the question.

My assumption is based on the following quote from the Medicare Payment Advisory Commission:

Large medical device companies are consistently profitable and typically have profit margins of 20 percent to 30 percent.

Therefore, I believe that the 15% margin is a conservative initial assumption when companies of similar quality in this industry typically range between 20% and 30%. And because TransMedics is still in the growth phase, I think it will take some time for them to reach the margin levels of the established players. But the potential is there.

The shares outstanding part is tricky. I am very confident that the shares outstanding in 2028 will be higher than they are now, but how much shareholders will be diluted is hard to estimate. But I think 40 million shares outstanding or maybe a little more is realistic.

Medical technology companies with a strong competitive advantage often have high multiples. And I definitely see TransMedics in the same boat as the peer group in the chart above in terms of quality of competitive advantage. TransMedics also has the advantage of being at the beginning of its journey, so its revenues are likely to continue to grow strongly through 2028, while the peer group is more likely to grow in the single digits.

Therefore, I believe the combination of TransMedics' near-monopoly and strong double-digit revenue growth justifies a 50x multiple. Intuitive Surgical, for example, had a multiple of 50x in 2009 and now, 15 years later, has a multiple of 83x. This means that this sector has the potential to reward high-quality companies with very high multiples over a longer period of time, in my opinion.

Revenue TTM $358m
Revenue 2028 $1.35b
Net Income (15% Margin) $202.5m
Shares Outstanding 40m
EPS $5.06
Multiple 50x
Share Price $253
Upside ~89%

With all this data, I have come to the conclusion that the stock has ~89% upside potential by the end of 2028. But I also think that my assumptions are quite conservative because the aviation part of the business could contribute a lot more to the total revenues, depending on how the fleet is expanded, and also because the net income margin could be more in the 20% range.

But even with my conservative assumptions, TransMedics looks like a compelling investment. And companies like Intuitive Surgical (ISRG) have shown that medical device companies that have a huge competitive advantage can grow strongly for years and trade at very high multiples for years.

Balance Sheet

Since TransMedics has only recently become profitable, my usual approach of comparing net income to long-term debt is not the right one for TransMedics. However, long term debt of nearly $507 million is countered by cash of $362 million. In addition, Q2/24 was the first positive FCF quarter with an excess of $2 million, and I expect FCF and interest coverage to grow rapidly in the coming months.

The liquidity situation seems to be in order, but is of course not as secure as with an established company that has been generating positive cash flows for years. So I think there is a little bit of risk there.

Risks

The above-mentioned liquidity situation, which offers a relatively low risk, can be added to the risk that competitors such as Xvivo (OTCPK:XVIPF), Organox or Lung Bioengineering could launch a similarly strong product. But even this risk I think is rather low at the moment because, as I said, clinical trials take a long time, and they would have to show better or similar results and those are not available yet.

Even xenotransplantation seems to be a long way off, as there have been first attempts, such as with David Bennett in 2022 and Richard Slayman this year, but the technology does not seem to be mature yet. But even if xenotransplantation continues to advance, opportunities for TransMedics could arise because here, too, it will be important to provide intact organs for as long and as well cooled as possible. Furthermore, I assume that most people would still prefer a human donor organ.

Conclusion

For me, TransMedics is a long-term buy-and-hold investment that I will probably hold for the next 10+ years. With such a long-time horizon, the competitive advantages are more important than the current multiple. But of course, I understand anyone with a shorter time horizon who says the company looks expensive right now because I also expect the price to be very volatile over the next few years.

But in conclusion, why I think TransMedics is attractive:

  • They operate in a market that they have revolutionized with their invention, creating strong competitive advantages and barriers to entry.
  • By becoming a trusted partner to donor and transplant centers, they can also build strong relationships and develop pricing power.
  • And a really big point is that this product really helps humanity.

data.title: BOE: Discount Opportunity Providing Global Exposure
data.description:
Written by Nick Ackerman, co-produced by Stanford Chemist

BlackRock Enhanced Global Dividend Trust (NYSE:BOE) is a closed-end fund providing investors with a portfolio with a tilt toward global exposure. The fund also incorporates a covered call writing strategy on a portion of its underlying individual positions. This helps to generate cash flow to provide an attractive monthly distribution to its investors.

It has been just over a year since our last update, and the fund has delivered some attractive total returns. That said, it has lagged when compared to the results of the S&P 500 Index. The S&P 500 isn't necessarily the appropriate benchmark due to the fund's global and covered call strategy, but it can provide some overall context.

That was despite the discount having narrowed a bit since this update as well, which would have been a positive contributor to the total returns. I think the fund's discount is still wide enough to be of interest, especially with the discount management program that was announced earlier this year.

BOE Basics

  • 1-Year Z-score: 1.88.
  • Discount/Premium: -11.69%.
  • Distribution Yield: 6.71%.
  • Expense Ratio: 1.09%.
  • Leverage: N/A.
  • Managed Assets: $764.7 million.
  • Structure: Perpetual.

BOE has an investment objective to "provide current income and current gains, with a secondary objective of long-term capital appreciation."

The fund intends to achieve this through the following:

...investing in at least 80% of its net assets in dividend-paying equity securities and at least 40% of its assets outside of the U.S. The Fund may invest in securities of companies of any market capitalization but intends to invest primarily in securities of large-capitalization companies. The Fund generally intends to write covered put and call options with respect to approximately 30% to 45% of its total assets. However, the percentage may vary from time to time with market conditions.

Performance - Attractive Discount Leads To Opportunity With Tender Offers

Earlier this year, the fund conducted a tender offer that was part of the fund's discount management program; with the next measurement period wrapping up now, the fund is set to conduct another tender offer, which is expiring for BOE on November 19, 2024.

The tender offer is triggered in any measurement period where the fund's discount averages -7.50% or wider. For BOE, that has been the case during this last measurement period and really or most of the last 10 years. There were only a couple of times that the fund trended toward a ~5% discount level but then quickly retreated lower.

This is a small offer at 2.5% of outstanding shares at 98% of NAV. However, as was the case with almost every tender offer, not everyone participates. That ends up seeing more than the 2.5% being accepted for the tender for investors who do participate.

In the case of BOE, the fund ended up accepting ~10.3% of investors' shares who elected to participate with 100% of their position. At 4x the 2.5% tender offer, that was a pretty solid outcome. The question now, after seeing several of the tender offers having significantly more shares accepted, is whether more investors will participate in the next round or not.

Historically speaking, the fund's performance hasn't been that great relative to funds that focus solely on U.S.-based investments. This has been an issue for a number of globally focused funds, as the U.S. equity space has been the clear outperformer for an extended period of time now.

However, while that has been the case for nearly two decades now, it has come with a cost more recently. That cost is the valuations in the U.S. have become quite elevated relative to their international counterparts. Should valuations start to matter again, or if the U.S. economy starts to slip up, we could be set up to underperform.

With having some added global exposure, it adds further diversification to one's portfolio. Another point to consider with BOE is that it isn't as heavily invested in the mega-cap tech names and there is more of a balance to its sector exposure. The fund still leaves heaviest in a tech allocation at 21.5%, but that can be compared to the S&P 500, which has seen its tech allocation climb to over 30% now.

With that said, the fund uses a benchmark of the MSCI ACWI and MSCI ACWI Call Overwrite Index, to help provide a better comparison. The MSCI ACWI Call Overwrite Index incorporates a 45% overwrite allocation. Unfortunately, BOE hasn't been able to keep pace with its benchmark historically, this can be the result of having operating expenses and also portfolio positioning.

Distribution - Could See A Boost

One area that negatively impacted the fund also being allocated to international investments that dragged down its relative performance was in the fund's distribution. The performance matters in terms of not only delivering dividends that can get passed onto shareholders, but as an equity fund, it will rely heavily on capital gains as well. That will either require appreciation from the underlying portfolio or collecting option premium from writing calls.

With weaker relative results, that meant a distribution that couldn't necessarily be supported without eroding NAV. That ultimately resulted in the fund cutting its distribution several times since its inception.

However, there is some optimism here as well, as I think they could use a distribution lift. Again, if international equities can start performing better, that means more fuel to fund the monthly distribution for investors.

Another sign though, that the distribution could use a boost is the actual distribution yield currently. Thanks to a hefty discount, the distribution rate on the share price comes to 6.71%. On an NAV basis, however, the distribution rate comes to an even more modest 5.93%. Over the last decade, that's what the fund has been able to deliver in terms of total NAV return results. Over the previous three and five-year periods annualized, the fund has been able to deliver beyond that rate by a fairly wide margin.

Of course, at the same time, whatever they don't pay out will simply go to the fund's NAV per share, and investors could potentially see appreciation. Looking at the latest semi-annual report, we can see exactly what the fund has been earning in terms of NII, and net realized/unrealized gains over the last several years. NII coverage in the latest report against the current monthly distribution works out to around 32%. For an equity fund, that isn't too unusual.

For tax purposes, the fund had seen a lot of its 2023 distribution classified as return of capital. We know this wasn't destructive to ROC as the NAV rose during the year, but they didn't realize a lot of those gains. In fact, they didn't realize any appreciation as they had a net realized loss for the year.

However, the year prior, they did sustain some significant losses in 2022. As of the end of 2023, the fund was still sitting on almost $25.4 million in capital loss carryforwards. That means we could see more ROC distributions this year as well, despite the NAV also climbing.

The remainder of the 2023 distribution was characterized as qualified dividend income. In general, the fund should see its distribution primarily as qualified dividend income and a return to long-term capital gains, assuming the fund continues to perform well. This would make it more appropriate for a taxable account as these classifications often see favorable tax-friendly treatments relative to ordinary income rates.

BOE's Portfolio

BOE delivers a global tilt, but it isn't entirely invested outside of the U.S. The U.S. alone still contributes to the largest single-country exposure, which is followed by primarily European countries after that. That includes the U.K., France, Switzerland, and Denmark as some larger weightings.

As noted above, the fund also isn't overly dominated by tech exposure-it is the largest exposure. Still, it isn't as weighty relative to what it has become for the broader market.

With broader market participation coming in over the last few months, we've seen that play out favorably for BOE. While the S&P 500 Index isn't the appropriate benchmark for a global fund that also utilizes covered calls, it can still provide some relative context.

Despite it being over a year since we last covered this fund, the actual portfolio sector allocations haven't changed too dramatically either. July 30 of last year saw information technology's weight at 19.06%, healthcare has slid lower some as it was previously the second-largest weighting at 18.85%. That is fairly material on a relative basis, but on an absolute basis, it hasn't been that much of a change. Financials were at 16.90% and industrials were at 14.45% at the time for comparison as well.

Several of the fund's largest holdings are also going to be familiar as being the same as last year as well, including Microsoft Corporation (MSFT), Accenture plc (ACN), AstraZeneca PLC (AZN), Apple Inc. (AAPL) and Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company Limited (TSM). Positions such as MSFT and AAPL are pretty much staples in almost any diversified equity CEF, as these are part of the Magnificent 7 names.

For 2023, the turnover rate came to 46%, which indicates that they are fairly active in their management. In the last semi-annual report, turnover came in at 28%, putting it on pace to be above last year's should the second half reflect the first.

Conclusion

BOE trades at a deep discount, and that has historically been the case. In fact, the current discount is right around the fund's decade-long average. However, what is different now is that the fund has implemented a discount management program where investors can participate in tender offers at 98% of NAV. They are offering to repurchase 2.5% of shares, which is quite small, but there are four measurement periods. Further, not every investor will participate, and we saw that over 10% of shares were accepted by investors who did participate with their full position.

With that said, while that provides a potential upside catalyst, it isn't risk-free. If investors jump in and buy a position now, and we enter into a correction, those upside gains would be reduced or even eliminated, resulting in a loss for investors participating. At the end of the day, one shouldn't be buying a position just to participate in the tender offer but because they want exposure to the types of underlying assets that BOE provides for investors.

data.title: Early Earnings Calls Indicate A Resilient U.S. Economy
data.description:
Earnings season has just begun, with 6% of the companies in the S&P 500 Index having reported earnings so far. S&P 500 earnings growth for the third quarter is currently expected to be 4.1%. However, there are significant differences between sectors - technology earnings growth expectations are 14.9%, while earnings growth for the materials and energy sectors are forecast to be negative.1 In addition to the financial data being reported, the transcripts from earnings calls are giving us some interesting insights into the economy as corporate executives share their observations and outlooks.

It's important to note that most S&P 500 companies have beaten expectations in previous quarters. In fact, the actual earnings growth rate for the index has exceeded the estimated earnings growth rate in 37 of the last 40 quarters.1 FactSet Research anticipates this will happen again this quarter, and that earnings growth will likely end up being around 7% year-over-year after actual earnings are reported for all index companies, well above the current estimate of 4.1%.1 This should be supportive of stocks.

Of course, I'm also interested in earnings season for what it can tell me about the strength of the economy (or lack thereof), risks to the economy, different sectors in the economy, different consumer segments, as well as the outlook for the near term. I went through the earnings call transcripts of a number of companies - both in the S&P 500 and outside it - that reported earnings last week. Here are some learnings from those calls:

  • The US economy remains in good shape, with a soft landing likely. As one major financial services company explained, "These results are consistent with a soft landing. That's pretty consistent with this kind of Goldilocks economic situation."2
  • In general, consumers are healthy. As the same major financial services company explained: "So overall, we see the spending patterns as being sort of solid, consistent with the narrative that the consumer is on solid footing and consistent with a strong labor market." 2 Another financial services company shared, "We continue to look for changes in consumer health, but we have not seen meaningful changes in trends when looking at delinquency statistics across our consumer credit portfolios."3 However, in general, consumers are continuing to be price conscious; several companies described them as "discerning"4 and other companies described them as seeking "value."5
  • Higher income consumers seem to still be powering the US economy. As a major airline carrier explained on its earnings call, "Consumers are continuing to prioritize premium experiences, and our core customer base is in a healthy financial position with travel remaining a top spend category."6 It seems that higher income consumers are compensating for weakness among lower income consumers. As explained on a financial services earnings call, "We continue to see more pronounced stress in certain customer segments with lower deposit and asset levels, where inflation has partially offset strong employment and wage growth."7 This makes sense, given that higher income consumers are a smaller portion of the population but are generally considered responsible for the majority of US consumer spending.
  • The labor market seems to be the key to the health of the consumer. As one financial services company articulated, "Overall, customers in our US consumer businesses continue to hold up relatively well, benefiting from a strong labor market and wage growth."8 Companies that have seen some consumer weakness attribute it to the labor market. One consumer beverage company explained, "...ongoing macroeconomic headwinds, particularly rising unemployment, have led to a recent deceleration in the rate of growth of consumer demand for our products..."9
  • Corporations are in good shape as well. As explained on a financial services company earnings call, "Company balance sheets are strong, contributing to both consumption and investment in the economy..."10 Companies seem to be feeling optimistic about next year. As the airline carrier explained, "Corporate travel continues to improve…"11 Their recent corporate survey showed 85% of respondents expect their travel spend to increase in 2025.11
  • We can't forget the economic risk created by a rise in trade wars. One industrial company shared that one reason for pressure on its margins was an increase in trade barriers, "…certain countries have raised import duties, lifting the cost to us of moving product across borders."12

All in all, these initial earnings calls have offered some confirmation of the resilience we've been seeing in recent US economic data - and a reminder of some of the risks.

More details emerge on China's stimulus plan

In other news, I've been talking about the great potential impact of the stimulus package Chinese policymakers announced several weeks ago. At a press conference over the weekend, we got more details from the Chinese Ministry of Finance about their plans to use the Ministry's balance sheet to support longer-term growth. While the specific amount of the new stimulus package wasn't provided, the plan calls for an increase in government debt to help local governments' balance sheets, re-capitalize state-owned banks, support the property market, and deliver financial support to groups of people in need. This is a comprehensive plan, and our initial take is that it's likely to benefit the economy and markets in the medium and longer term with the intention of making positive structural changes.

Looking ahead

This week will bring more earnings reports and some key data, including US retail sales and US industrial production - which mean more insight into the strength of the US economy. In addition, we'll get reports on Canada and UK inflation as well as eurozone economic sentiment among other data releases.

As I read the earnings call transcripts, I will be focused on any guidance around job cuts. As I mentioned above, it seems the strength of the US consumer going forward will be dictated by the strength of the labor market. Despite some recent positive economic data, I am ever more vigilant in looking for cracks in the US economy, the epicenter of which would likely be a weakening in the labor market. With initial US jobless claims spiking last week to the highest level in more than a year, I will be looking for any signs of weakening in the labor market - since it arguably holds the key to the health of the US consumer, which in turn holds the key to the health of the US economy

data.title: Ultralife Corporation: A Battery Stock Charging Up For A Comeback
data.description:
Ultralife Corporation (NASDAQ:ULBI) is just a week from releasing Q3 earnings results, on October 24.

Considering the 25% decline since the release of Q2 results, investors are wondering if now is a good time to initiate a long position in this stock.

In my view, considering the cheap valuation ratios and the strong fundamentals of the company, accompanied by a jump in interest from institutional investors, I believe the upside outweighs the risks that the company is currently facing.

In the outlook section, I will provide the rationale behind my Buy rating in this stock.

For now, I would like to begin with a brief company overview section for those readers new to this stock.

Company Overview

Imagine you're out on a remote military mission. Your communication device needs to stay charged, your backup power system needs to be reliable, and the equipment you use to send signals has to work in all weather conditions. Ultralife Corporation is the company behind making sure all that happens.

This is a US-based company with a portfolio of products ranging from rechargeable and non-rechargeable batteries to charging systems, and communication electronics, serving commercial, government, and defense markets worldwide.

Therefore, the company has two main business segments: battery & energy, and communication systems. I considered including below a breakdown of the YTD revenue from their latest 10-Q.

Year Battery & Energy Products Communications Systems Total Revenue
2024 (YTD, June) $71.67M $13.24M $84.91M
2023 (YTD, June) $62.33M $12.28M $74.61M

As already mentioned, they operate internationally, so I included below a breakdown of YTD revenue by geographical area.

Year United States Revenue Non-United States Revenue Total Revenue
2024 (YTD, June) $47.86M $37.05M $84.91M
2023 (YTD, June) $37.98M $36.62M $74.61M

Ownership-wise, the latest 14A shows a beneficial ownership of 40% among all 6 directors and executive officers. I am highly encouraged about this figure, as I favor companies where management has skin in the game.

Headwinds, Risks, And Challenges

Let's start with dessert.

The communication systems segment is taking a big hit, with a 28.7% YoY revenue decline in this segment. The main driver was delays in shipments to defense contractors that were supposed to occur in earlier periods but were pushed back due to ongoing supply chain disruptions.

These orders were mainly vehicle amplifier adapters and radio vehicle mounts. Although management didn't provide full details during the earnings call, or in the Q2 earnings report, it seems (see section 2 of this report) that there are industry-wise shortages for semiconductors, electronic components, and raw materials including aluminum and copper, which are used in military communications systems.

It appears that the supply chain disruptions are external to the company, so I am not overly concerned about this issue.

However, the backlog decline is concerning. In Q2 2023, the company's backlog stood at $108 million. Today, it stands at $93 million, a 14% decline YoY. In my view, the delays in the communication systems segment could have played a big role in this decline, with defense contractors easing off new orders until the supply chain disruptions are sorted out.

Aside from the revenue decline in this segment, gross profit also declined by a significant amount. Specifically, in Q2 2024, the communication systems segment's gross profit declined by 890 bps YoY. The main driver was an unfavorable product mix, mainly due to these disruptions.

It appears that the vehicle amplifier adapters have high gross margins. Therefore, due to the delays in the supply chain, shipments of these high-margin products were replaced by lower-margin items, leading to a decline in gross margin.

Moving on to other pressures, oil and gas market sales experienced a 10.9% decline in Q2 2024. Management attributed this decline to overbuying activity in Q2 2023, which led to an inventory correction during the last quarter. Additionally, the volatility of the natural gas sector might have contributed to this revenue decline.

Finally, I see a moderate risk due to the high reliance on US government and defense contracts in the battery & energy segment. In Q2 2024, 30.5% of battery & energy Products sales growth was attributed to US government and defense contracts alone.

With the upcoming U.S. election, changes in government policies could affect the growth rate of this segment.

Outlook

I'd like to start with price action.

As shown in the daily chart below, since May, the share price has declined by over 33%. Additionally, there appears to be an upward trend, although I don't consider the trend line to be a reliable support level.

If we compare the return of Ultralife to its sector, industry, and the S&P 500 over the past 2 years, it appears that returns are very volatile, with an outperformance in May this year, and underperformance during the first half of 2023.

So, considering the 33% decline since May, does it make sense to initiate a long position in this stock? In my view, the answer is yes, although my conviction is not very high. Therefore, I maintain a Buy rating, instead of a Strong Buy. Let me explain below why.

A quick look at the income statement shows consistent revenue growth YoY since March 2022.

Gross income and operating income growth follow. However, net income has declined by 11% in the last quarter.

Despite the 11% decline in net income, I am not worried, due to the fact that the company benefited in Q2 2023 from a $1.544 million Employee Retention Credit, which significantly boosted "other income" in the income statement.

As a side note, if you are wondering about the non-operating income from Q2 2024, Ultralife Corporation received $235,000 in insurance payments related to a ransomware cyberattack that occurred in Q1 2023.

Moving on to the balance sheet, there was a significant decline in net debt in the last quarter. The company reduced borrowings on its revolving credit facility by $12.68 million and made scheduled payments of $1 million on its term loan.

Additionally, I am encouraged by the improvement in tangible book value per share.

With regard to the debt structure, I have included below a summary using the latest earnings report.

Debt Type Amount Maturity Date Interest Rate Interest Type Annual Principal Payments
Term Loan Facility $5.167 million January 1, 2027 Daily Simple SOFR + 0.10% + 2.10-2.40% Variable $1 million
Revolving Credit Facility $6.902 million May 30, 2028 Daily Simple SOFR + 0.10% + 2.10-2.40% Variable N/A (interest-only until maturity)

As a side note, the revolving credit facility can be increased to $50 million up to six months before the maturity date.

Their liquidity ratios look really good, so I am not concerned with their ability to pay down debt.

Liquidity Ratio Value
Current Ratio 4.07
Quick Ratio 2.06
Cash Ratio 0.33

Moving now to the cash flow statement, I am encouraged by the improvement in the last quarter.

It appears that the company has finally managed to improve its working capital, with accounts receivables leading to a positive impact of $654k compared to the negative impact of $803k in H1 2023. Additionally, accounts payable and other liabilities decreased by $2.56 million, compared to a $413k increase in 2023. Finally, there was also an improvement in inventory management, with a positive cash inflow of $717k in H1 2024, compared to a $4.88 million cash outflow in the same period last year.

Moving on to insider buying activity, there were only two transactions in 2024, but they were very small in size, so they don't provide any relevant signal.

However, one of the activist investors, Whitmore Bradford, increased his ownership in the company by 7.2% in September this year. In my view, this is encouraging, considering that he bought on a day when shares declined by 7.7%. My investment style favors this type of contrarian buying activity.

A quick look at institutional investor activity shows an increase of 17.8% in the total shares reported in 13F filings and a 67% decline in the put/call ratio. In my view, there is a bullish sentiment among institutional investors, considering that the number of new positions during the last quarter increased by 42%.

Moving on to valuation, I believe the company is undervalued. A quick look at the football field chart below shows most of the valuation ratios trading close to the 5-year minimum.

Additionally, when comparing these ratios to some competitors' the shares look cheap. An example is shown below, where the table is sorted in descending order by P/TB value.

Conclusion

To wrap up, I believe Ultralife Corporation presents a compelling opportunity at its current valuation.

Over the last 2 years, the company has shown consistent revenue growth, particularly in the battery and energy products segment, which benefits from strong demand in both commercial and defense markets.

The significant improvement in working capital, debt reduction, and a strong liquidity position further strengthens my bull case. With institutional investors increasing their stakes and the stock trading near five-year valuation lows, I see potential for meaningful upside, particularly if they manage to mitigate the recent supply chain disruptions.

However, there are some risks to be aware of, particularly in the communication systems segment, where revenue declined by 28.7% in the last year. Additionally, the reliance on U.S. government contracts introduces a level of uncertainty, especially with potential changes in policy following upcoming elections.

To conclude, while Ultralife faces some headwinds, I believe the fundamentals and the cheap valuation ratios outweigh the risks.

data.title: Tiny TEN Holdings Pursues IPO On Uneven Results And Excessive Valuation
data.description:
TEN Holdings Seeks IPO Amid Losses And High Valuation

TEN Holdings, Inc. (XHLD) has filed to raise $12.5 million in an IPO of its common stock, according to an SEC S-1 registration statement.

TEN Holdings plans and produces virtual, physical and hybrid events via its proprietary platform.

The firm is still quite tiny and is generating continued high operating losses for its size, and its valuation assumptions at IPO are unreasonable.

My opinion on the IPO is to sell (avoid it).

What Does TEN Holdings Do?

Langhorne, Pennsylvania-based TEN Holdings, Inc. was founded to develop its Xyvid Pro Platform that assists organizations in planning, producing and broadcasting their virtual, physical and hybrid events.

Management is headed by president and CEO Randolph Wilson Jones III, who has been with the firm since 2023 and was previously chief revenue officer at Anodot and at ScienceLogic.

The company intends to focus its efforts on increasing its business growth efforts, enhancing its technology, and broadening its service offerings.

As of June 30, 2024, TEN Holdings has booked a fair market value investment of $5,000 from investors, including V-Cube, Inc. and Eastern Nationals Trading Pte. Ltd.

The firm seeks customers of all sizes for its webcasting services via its direct sales and marketing efforts and through word-of-mouth referrals.

Management touted its repeat customer success. For the calendar year 2023, eight of its top ten customers in terms of revenue were repeat clients.

Selling, G&A expenses as a function of total revenue have risen materially as revenues have increased, as the figures below indicate:

Assuming a successful IPO, the company's enterprise value at IPO would approximate $127.4 million, excluding the effects of underwriter over-allotment options.

The float to outstanding shares ratio (assuming no underwriter over-allotments) will be approximately 9.1%.

Management disclosed it intends to apply the net proceeds from the IPO as follows:

approximately 20% of the net proceeds we receive from this offering, for research and development, focusing on advanced technologies like artificial intelligence and cloud streaming capabilities, to enhance our platform;

approximately 20% of the net proceeds we receive from this offering, for marketing and sales efforts, including digital campaigns and expanding our sales team, to boost brand awareness and customer acquisition;

approximately 15% of the net proceeds we receive from this offering, for working capital and general corporate purposes, to support day-to-day operations and future growth initiatives;

approximately 15% of the net proceeds we receive from this offering for the prepayment of short-term - related party debt from a majority stockholder, which indebtedness has an interest rate of 6% and a maturity date of December 31, 2024, and the uses of proceeds of which indebtedness are for working capital needs and pre-IPO costs;

approximately 15% of the net proceeds we receive from this offering, for potential future acquisitions, although as of the date of this prospectus, we have not identified, nor have we engaged in any material discussions regarding, any potential target subject to such acquisitions or investments; and

approximately 15% of the net proceeds we receive from this offering, for infrastructure and technology upgrades, to improve scalability, security, and performance of our technology and our systems.

(Source - SEC)

The company's online roadshow is not available.

Regarding outstanding legal proceedings, the company said it is not aware of any pending or threatened legal actions against the firm that would have a 'major impact' on its business.

The only listed book runner of the IPO is Bancroft Capital, LLC.

TEN Holdings Is A Tiny Firm With Significant Operating Losses

XHLD is seeking U.S. public investment for its various working capital and expansion requirements.

The company's financials have generated fluctuating topline revenue, variable gross profit and gross margin, higher trending operating losses and higher cash used in operations.

Free cash flow for the trailing twelve-month period ended June 30, 2024, was negative ($2.3 million).

Selling, G&A expenses as a percentage of total revenue increased significantly as revenue has grown from a small base; its Selling, G&A efficiency multiple rose to 0.2x, a low figure.

The firm currently plans to pay zero dividends and intends to keep any earnings for its future growth plans.

XHLD's recent capital spending history shows it has spent heavily on capital expenditures even as it has generated negative operating cash flow.

The market opportunity for live-streaming events is large and expected to grow at a high rate of growth in the coming years, so TEN enjoys strong industry growth dynamics in its favor.

However, it faces substantial competition from established industry players and the firm is thinly capitalized.

data.title: Uber: The Big Winner Of Tesla's 'We, Robot' Flop
data.description:
Tesla Flop, Uber Top

Uber (NYSE:UBER) shares surged almost 11% following Tesla's 'We, Robot' event, while Tesla (TSLA) shares closed the trading day almost 9% lower. In my view, this is a clear investor vote of how Tesla's progress (or lack of progress) in autonomous driving is set to influence the business model of these two mobility companies: Uber top, Tesla flop.

In my view, the contrasting stock movements signify a broader reassessment of the timelines and feasibility of autonomous vehicle integration into mainstream transportation. Tesla has long been at the forefront of promoting a future dominated by self-driving cars, with bold claims about imminent technological breakthroughs since 2016. Although Musk presented the concept of a Robovan and a Cybercab for less than $30k, the 'We, Robot' event also reinforced many investor's projection (mine included) that Tesla is still years away from bringing the technology to market. Indeed, Elon Musk himself said that the Cybercab won't go into commercialization mode until 2027:

We expect to be in production with the Cybercab, which is highly optimized for autonomous transport in probably - I tend to be a little optimistic with timeframes - but in 2026; before 2027 let me put it that way

And if Musk says an event is two years away, a cynic may argue the event is actually two years away from being two years away. Meanwhile, Tesla's slow progress in autonomous driving opens opportunities for Uber to solidify its role as a mobility leader.

Uber's Partnership Approach Makes Sense

Interestingly, Uber may be a winner in autonomous driving revolution, without carrying the risk of developing the technology (like Tesla). In fact, Uber's strategy for commercializing autonomous driving centers on partnerships and integrations rather than in-house development. After selling its self-driving unit to Aurora Innovation in 2020, Uber invested in the company and continues to collaborate with autonomous vehicle firms to incorporate their technology into its platform. This approach allows Uber to leverage advancements in autonomous driving while focusing on its core services, aiming to seamlessly integrate self-driving vehicles into its existing network to enhance efficiency and reduce operational costs over time. As of 2024, Uber has announced partnerships with major players like Waymo, Avride, and Cruise to integrate autonomous technologies into its platform. These collaborations not only enhance Uber's technological capabilities but also spread the financial risk and development costs associated with autonomous driving. For instance, Waymo has begun launching its autonomous driving in select cities, and Uber plans a broader rollout in 2025.

Uber Is Leading In Mobility-As-A-Service (MAAS)

While Tesla is struggling to commercialize MAAS through technology, Uber is strengthening its global MAAS footprint with robust commercial momentum. For context, in Q2 the company reported $10.7 billion in total revenue, a 15% YoY increase. The Mobility segment grew by 25% to $6.1 billion, mostly driven by an expansion in ride volumes. Uber's EBITDA surged 71% to $1.6 billion, marking the fifth consecutive quarter of positive adjusted EBITDA, while net income more than doubled to $796 million.

Riding Smoothly Towards $11 Billion Of FCF By 2027

Without any disruption to Uber's momentum, the company is on track to generate close to $11 billion of free cash flow by 2027, according to analyst consensus estimates by Refintiv. If these estimates are accurate, Uber would currently be trading at only 16x 2027 free cash flow. And as Uber is likely to distribute close to 100% of free cash flow to shareholders, given the minimal capital/CAPEX need for operating its platform, the implied trading on 2027 equity yield may be 6%.

Risks To The Thesis

While Uber's 11% surge reflects growing investor confidence in its current business model amidst slower progress in autonomous driving, this upside carries significant risks. The company's reliance on the traditional ride-hailing model could create a false sense of security, leading to complacency in preparing for the inevitable arrival of autonomous vehicles. In the long term, this could threaten Uber's market position and profitability, especially if autonomous technology proves to be disruptive faster than anticipated.

Conclusion

The significant divergence in stock performance between Uber and Tesla following Tesla's 'We, Robot' event highlights investor sentiment regarding the progress and viability of autonomous driving technology. Tesla's shares dropped nearly 9%, suggesting a collective disappointment in the company's advancements-or perceived lack thereof-in self-driving capabilities, leading to concerns about Tesla's ability to fulfill its ambitious promises in the near future. Conversely, Uber's shares surged almost 11%, indicating growing investor confidence in the company's current business model amidst the slower-than-expected progress in autonomous driving. The stagnation in Tesla's self-driving advancements suggests that the threat of autonomous vehicles disrupting ride-hailing services is less immediate than previously thought. This means that Uber can continue to capitalize on its existing, booming operations without the pressing need to pivot or compete directly with self-driving technology.

data.title: The State Of U.S.-China Trade As New Tariffs Take Effect
data.description:
After July 2024's tariff-clock beating surge, the total value of trade recorded between the U.S. and China coasted slightly higher in August 2024, even as its momentum reversed.

With the Biden-Harris administration's new and expanded set of tariffs taking effect during the month, the negative change in momentum was expected. Importers of goods from China had rushed to pull as many deliveries of goods subject to the new tariffs ahead as possible, which by itself will diminish the level of trade in the months ahead. Although these imports typically peak in October each year, the actions to avoid the Biden-Harris administration's tariffs likely moved 2024's peak to July. U.S. imports of goods from China fell in August.

The total value of trade was up in August 2024, however, because U.S. exports of agricultural goods like soybeans to China rose. U.S. exports of these goods to China are typically elevated during the last quarter of the year, but were elevated year-over-year.

The following chart shows how the August 2024 surge impacted the total value of goods traded between the U.S. and China.

This chart shows the nominal level of the total value of goods exchanged between the U.S. and China over the period from January 2017 through August 2024. Unfortunately, it doesn't fully convey the effects of the tariffs and other events on the level of U.S. and China trade.

For that, we need to take the effects of inflation into account. In the next chart, we've done that for the combined value of goods imported and exported between the U.S. and China, which are expressed in terms of constant 2017 U.S. dollars.

The level of trade between the U.S. and China peaked at $54.2 billion in 2017 USD in September 2018, when the Trump administration initiated a tariff war with China (Event A). The level of trade dropped 18.4% to $44.8 billion in 2017 USD, which would have marked a bottom given the December 2017 Phase 1 trade deal between the U.S. and China, but didn't because of the onset of the coronavirus pandemic (Event B). The pandemic took the inflation-adjusted bottom of trade between the U.S. and China down to its Trump-era low of $42.8 billion in April 2020, some 21.1% below its September 2018 peak (Event C).

After April 2020, the level of trade grew rapidly, until a period of high inflation for the U.S. economy with the passage of the American Rescue Plan Act of 2021 in March 2021 (Event D). The high inflation in this period stalled the recovery in trade between the two nations, which ranged between $50 and $51 billion through September 2022.

That coincides with the peak in trade ahead of the Biden-Harris administration's announcement of its own tariffs and export restrictions on U.S. semiconductor technologies, which crashed the amount of trade between the U.S. and China. The level of trade plunged $9.8 from $50.9 billion in September 2022 to bottom at $41.1 billion in May 2024.

At this point, the Biden-Harris administration's trade sanctions against China have been more damaging in inflation-adjusted terms than the Trump administration's non-pandemic affected sanctions. However, in May 2024, the Biden-Harris administration announced new and expanded tariffs and other trade sanctions in May 2024 (Event E), which started taking effect in August 2024 and is already negatively affecting the trade data. That impact will continue in the months ahead.

data.title: ClearBridge Small Cap Strategy Q3 2024 Commentary
data.description:
By Albert Grosman & Brian Lund, CFA

Small Caps Rebound on Rate Cut

Market Overview

Despite causing surges in market volatility during the quarter, a series of weaker than anticipated labor reports and subdued inflation readings helped pave the way for the Fed to cut interest rates by 50 basis points in September, its first cut since the beginning of the COVID-19 pandemic. The likelihood of further cuts and access to cheaper and easier to obtain financing also acted as an additional tailwind to the rotation away from the generative AI beneficiaries that dominated the first half of the year and toward broader market leadership favoring small cap stocks.

As a result, the benchmark Russell 2000 Index (RTY) returned 9.27%, handily outperforming both the S&P 500 Index (SP500, SPX) and the large cap Russell 1000 Index, which returned 5.89% and 6.08%, respectively. The rotation away from IT companies and into other sectors of the market also helped bolster the performance of value stocks, with the Russell 2000 Value Index returning 10.15%, 174 basis points ahead of the Russell 2000 Growth Index.

Portfolio Performance

The ClearBridge Small Cap Strategy underperformed its benchmark in the third quarter, as detractors in the communication services and information technology sectors overcame strong performance by several of our health care and consumer discretionary holdings.

Stock selection in the communication services sector was a significant detractor during the period, largely due to not owning Lumen Technologies (LUMN), which provides products and services including dark fiber, edge cloud services and internet protocol, among others. The company, which began the quarter with a $1.1 billion market cap, skyrocketed after it signed agreements with Microsoft (MSFT) and Corning (GLW) to use its network and technologies to support their AI data center buildouts, resulting in a nearly 350% return and ending the quarter with a $7.2 billion market cap. However, despite this meteoric rise, we believe that the company remains a highly risky asset with a significantly leveraged balance sheet, and one not suitable for our focus on high-quality, long-term compounders.

Additionally, Vivid Seats (SEAT), which operates as an online secondary marketplace for tickets in the U.S. and Canada, came under pressure for reducing its full-year 2024 guidance due to competitors ramping up their competitive pricing. However, we have conviction in management's stance to not pursue volume at the expense of profits, and we are encouraged by Vivid Seats exceeding market expectations for second-quarter earnings. We believe the company's strong balance sheet will allow it to persist through its peers' short-sighted strategies.

Stock selection in the IT sector also detracted from performance, with the broadening of market participation into other sectors proving additional headwinds to several of our holdings. This included Bigcommerce (BIGC), which provides a software platform that helps enable e-commerce. Struggles in the company's go-to-market execution weighed on performance during the quarter, further extending its turnaround. Another IT holding, Indie Semiconductor (INDI), saw its stock price fall as a sharp contraction in the supply chain for the auto industry has caused the company's revenue growth to stall. As a provider of automotive semiconductors and software, including for applications such as driver assistance systems, connected cars and electrification, the company has been further hampered by pushouts of new car models. Despite these near-term headwinds, Indie Semiconductor continues to have a strong backlog of orders and, as the automotive supply chain normalizes, we believe the company should see acceleration in revenue growth and share gains.

"Rather than gambling on an election's outcome, we apply a probabilistic approach to evaluate a wide range of possibilities."

Stock selection in the health care sector was the greatest contributor to relative performance, with three of our top five individual contributors coming from the sector. Lantheus (LNTH), which makes diagnostic and therapeutic products that assist clinicians in the diagnosis and treatment of heart disease, cancer and other diseases, rose sharply in the third quarter after the Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services proposed changes to its reimbursement policies for radiopharmaceutical products like Lantheus's Pylarify. This allows Pylarify to continue to receive temporary pass-through reimbursement through 2026, mitigating investor concerns that this reimbursement would cease at the end of this year.

Another top contributor in health care, Corcept Therapeutics (CORT), saw its stock rise in the third quarter after strong earnings results and increased guidance for sales of Korlym, a hyperglycemia drug for adults with Cushing's syndrome, which investors had been concerned would be vulnerable to the launch of a competitor's generic version late last year. The company has seen added tailwinds from anticipation surrounding its next-generation version of the drug, which has fewer side effects, and is expected to release phase 3 data later this year and potentially file for FDA approval in 2025.

The consumer discretionary sector also contributed due to positive performance from stocks like Stride (LRN), which provides curriculum, software, and educational solutions created for online delivery to students in kindergarten through 12th grade. The company's share price was buoyed by strong quarterly results, as it continues to attract and retain an increasing number of students, with enrollment growth continuing to surprise on the upside. In addition to its strong performance trajectory, we believe that the company's unique offerings such as its growing Career Learning programs continue to be compelling opportunities to create further value.

Looking To What Comes Next

American writer Ursula K. Le Guin once wrote, "The only thing that makes life possible is permanent, intolerable uncertainty; not knowing what comes next." However, this hasn't stopped investors and financial markets from trying to do just that with whatever topic or trend is fast approaching. In the 2000s it was "what comes next for the internet." After the Global Financial Crisis (GFC) it was "what comes next for real estate." And in the wake of COVID-19 it was, well, "what comes next for everything." Having finally settled the question of what comes next regarding the rate cut cycle with the Fed's 50 basis point cut in September, the new "what comes next" speculation is the looming U.S. presidential election between Vice President Harris and former President Trump - and particularly what the different permutations of electoral math would mean for the economy and the markets.

We are paying particularly close attention to the candidates' approaches regarding taxes and tariffs. In terms of tax policy, both candidates have proposals aimed at reducing taxes for service workers and the middle class but differ in that Harris suggests increasing taxes on high-income earners while Trump has advocated extending tax cuts to the wealthy. In terms of tariffs, a Harris presidency would likely continue to follow the path set by the current administration whereas Trump has proposed a bevy of new, protection-oriented tariffs which could cost middle class households approximately $2,600 USD per year and act as a de-facto tax - counterbalancing many of the benefits of reduced taxes.1 Additionally, immigration remains a contentious issue, with possible implications across a wide range of manpower-dependent sectors such as consumer staples and industrials should changes in policy result in more stringent entry requirements and an increase in deportations.

It can be difficult to avoid falling into the trap of speculating what a win one way or another would mean for the investors, particularly as the small cap market's discounting function - which provides a glimpse into how the broader market anticipates the changing fortunes of publicly traded companies - has been potentially hampered by a decline in actively managed small cap assets, which have declined from 80% of small cap funds twenty years ago to below 50% today as passive ETF grow larger.

This substantial decline in actively traded assets in the small cap universe over the last two decades has had greater implications beyond merely a lack of transparency into the collective sentiment in the market. For instance, the highly anticipated September employment report - closely watched to gauge the likelihood and strength of the Fed's anticipated rate cut - caused a spike in market volatility akin to those seen during the much more substantial upheavals of the Dot-Com burst, the GFC and the COVID-19 pandemic (Exhibit 1). Likewise, we are seeing this kind of increased volatility surrounding earnings season as a smaller number of active players making decisions are being amplified and followed by large passive funds. This was especially prevalent in the third quarter, which proved the most volatile earnings season since the GFC, with the average S&P 500 stock seeing an approximately 5% change in its price during earnings season - the largest aggregate change in over a decade.

Exhibit 1: September Employment Spurs Elevated Volatility

Coming full circle, these recent and outsize shocks stemming from the results of relatively routine events means that it has become even more difficult for the market and investors to determine "what comes next." We are, of course, not immune to the impact and the implications that different tax policies, regulatory regimes and trade decisions would have on small cap companies. However, rather than gambling on an election's outcome, we continue to apply a probabilistic approach in evaluating a wide range of possibilities and constructing our portfolio around strong companies with good balance sheets that are the most likely to persevere and prevail against the widest range of outcomes.

Portfolio Positioning

Our largest new position during the quarter was Meritage Homes (MTH), in the consumer discretionary sector, which builds single family attached and detached homes in the western and southeastern U.S. While higher mortgage rates have weighed on homebuilders and the demand for housing, we continue to believe that there is a systematic shortage of housing in the U.S., and that a decline in interest rates and subsequent mortgage rates should prove a boon to homebuilders. Beyond broader sector tailwinds, we opted to move our capital from Century Communities (CCS) to Meritage given the latter's higher historical level of returns at a comparable current valuation.

We also added a new position in Hain Celestial (HAIN), in the consumer staples sector, which makes organic and natural products including infant formula, frozen desserts, plant-based beverages such as soy, rice and oat and frozen meat alternatives among others. Hain has seemingly been written off by the market, but new management has launched a company-wide turnaround effort that we believe will result in a more focused, profitable and growing company.

We exited our position in Everi (EVRI), in the consumer discretionary sector, which provides games, cash access and customer relationship technologies to the casino industry. The company announced its intention to be acquired during the period by Apollo Global Management (APO) and, as we did not anticipate a better offer for the company, we elected to sell the position.

Outlook

Despite our disciplined, bottom-up, fundamentals-based approach to investing, we recognize that large macro events such as the upcoming election will result in a lot of speculative "what's next" for companies, their valuations and the broader direction of the market. However, as the collective wisdom of the market becomes more opaque due to the increased presence and outsize impact of passively managed and index funds, we believe that it is even more important for investors to adhere to a disciplined investment process to navigate the increase in these short-term volatility spikes. By employing a probabilistic approach, married with a focus on constructing a portfolio of strong companies with excellent balance sheets and compelling individual catalysts, we believe that our process is well-equipped for the long-term, but we acknowledge that the ride will not always be a smooth one.

Portfolio Highlights

The ClearBridge Small Cap Strategy underperformed its Russell 2000 Index benchmark during the third quarter. On an absolute basis, the Strategy had gains in nine of the 11 sectors in which it was invested during the quarter. The leading contributors were the health care and financials sectors, while the energy and communication services sectors detracted.

On a relative basis, stock selection in the health care, consumer discretionary and materials sectors also proved beneficial to performance. Conversely, stock selection in the communication services, financials, IT, industrials and real estate sectors and an overweight to the energy sector weighed on performance.

On an individual stock basis, the biggest contributors to absolute returns in the quarter were Everi, Lantheus, Tecnoglass (TGLS), Corcept Therapeutics and Verona Pharma (VRNA). The largest detractors were Valaris (VAL), Vivid Seats, Matador Resources (MTDR), Janus International (JBI) and Bigcommerce.

In addition to the transactions listed above, we initiated new positions in Guardian Pharmacy Services (GRDN) and Helen of Troy (HELE) in the consumer discretionary sector, Tarsus Pharmaceuticals (TARS), Insmed (INSM) and Vaxcyte (PCVX) in the health care sector, WSFS Financial (WSFS) in the financials sector, Expro (XPRO) in the energy sector and Silgan (SLGN) in the materials sector. We exited positions in Helmerich & Payne (HP) and Magnolia Oil & Gas (MGY) in the energy sector, Avient (AVNT) and Constellium (CSTM) in the materials sector, Centuri (CTRI) in the industrials sector, Group 1 Automotive (GPI) in the consumer discretionary sector, Advanced Energy Industries (AEIS) in the IT sector, PLAYSTUDIOS (MYPS) in the communication services sector and R1 RCM (RCM) in the health care sector.

Albert Grosman, Managing Director, Portfolio Manager

data.title: WPP plc: Best Sector Value After FGS Global Sale Creates Precedent
data.description:
Of the several listed advertising and PR agency conglomerations on the public markets, WPP plc (NYSE:WPP) is currently trading at a discount. There are reasons for the discount. Growth has been downgraded, APAC exposure is greater than peers, although same market performance looks alright. There has been a lot of lip service to AI, which we don't see as an edge for WPP over peers who probably have the same plans. Some things might get easier into the end of the year in some segments like tech, comps are going to be easier against the figures at the end of 2023, and the cost savings plan in action could unfold nicely. Still, challenges remain. However, there is a value case to be made for WPP given the precedent now for the partial sale of FGS Global, one of their PR businesses, to KKR (KKR). We aren't crazy about the whole industry, which is very mature also at this point, so if we had to pick, we'd probably go with WPP, which at least has some relative value.

Latest Earnings

The company expects no growth this year, with LFL revenue supposed to come in at a flat rate.

In the latest quarter, revenue growth improved relatively in line with peers in the US, but is still down for the HY. The issue is that overall Q2 revenue performance was really weakened by the pretty outsized skew that WPP has towards APAC. APAC's downward revenue trajectory continues, and this is consistent with a macro environment that is being regionally helmed by China's relative decline as its property market goes through a painful deleveraging. A deleveraging is not an ordinary correction, it is protracted, and government action has not really been an aggressive bail out of the market and therefore the pressures on household wealth, tied overwhelmingly up in property values, as well as household cash flows which are also disproportionately tied up in a massive real estate industry that dominates GDP, are straining demand. The expectation is that the second half will remain "very challenging" in China, with no real sign of improvement, and likely to be down double digits by the end of the full year. China is a big part of the APAC exposure, so there is reason for concern.

The tech clients haven't been spending a lot this H1 compared to last year's, which was a tougher comp. Comps are getting easier towards the end of the year and also the spend itself is stabilizing and maybe even going to grow sequentially. This could become a tailwind that offsets the pressure from China and is expected to support the guidance for flat growth in revenues.

Current EPS is around 30p per share, which annualized to around 60p assuming no margin improvement. However, there is a cost plan underway for a 125 million GBP in operating profit improvements, which is 10% of annualized operating profits. More than 60% of the plan is expected to be achieved by the end of 2024. Including that as a 6% bump to EPS annualized for 2024 puts the forward PE figure at around 12x, which is around in line with the forward PEs of Omnicom (OMC) and Interpublic Group of Companies (IPG) as given by Seeking Alpha. However, both of those companies have significantly smaller APAC exposure in the mix, with IPG being, for example, mostly focused on the US, which remains a pretty solid consumer market with macro not too unfavorable.

Bottom Line

WPP also sold a stake in its FGS Global business that is focused on providing PR services. The multiple looks quite nice, and the 600 million GBP in cash proceeds after tax are expected to be put towards deleveraging which will have a favorable impact on finance costs, enough possibly to bring forward PEs down to below 12x on a forward basis. The annual operating profit (basically EBITDA due to low D&A) of FGS is around 66 million GBP and the business was valued at around 1.3 billion GBP. It's a great multiple at around 20x EV/EBITDA. Comprehensive WPP EV/EBITDA is closer to around 6.2x, and FGS is only half of the annualized EBITDA from the PR business which is a segment that before the partial sale of FGS accounts for around 10% of overall WPP EBITDA. WPP EV is around 11 billion GBP, and the PR business valued at FGS' multiple actually accounts for around 29% of the EV at a little over 12% of the EBITDA. The residual multiple on the advertising agencies and franchises is something like 5.2x when including the full 125 million GBP in cost savings as well expected by end of 2025 in the annual run rate. IPG comprehensively is valued at something like 7.2x EV/EBITDA with its own non-PR businesses, mainly advertising of various kinds like WPP, valued at around 6.4x (with IPG also similarly seeing around 10% of revenue and 12% or so of EBITDA from PR businesses). WPP's businesses look pretty undervalued by around 20% in terms of multiple. Of course, the APAC skew is something not to like about WPP, and we think there is going to be a protracted pressure there, but with the industry altogether being pretty unattractive and mature, we'd rather go with the discount issue here and target some value.
